California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmlssron
Karen Bra i-nerd
Legislative Assistant
Respect Our An j,mal- Rights
915 rrLrt Street, Suite 1000
Sacramento, CA 9 5814
December 27, l99o
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File Nos. r-9o-413 and I-90-551
l/ 
Governrnent code Sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory
references are to the covernnent Code un].ess otherwise indicated.
cornmission regulations appear at 2 californj-a code of Regulations
Sect j,on 1BOOO; et seq. i11 references to regulations are to Title
. 
2, Division 6 of the california code of Regulations.
Dear Ms. Brainerd:
You have requested advice on behalf of Respect our Animals'
Rights (nROARtr) and Shirley Wechsler, a member of ROAR's board of
directors, under the campaign reporting provisions of the
Political 
Reform Act (the "Act"1.r7 Because your request i's a
general inquiry, \.re are treating your request as one for inforrnal
issistance -pur-suant to Regulati;n- 18329 (;) (copy enclosed) -21
ASEETI.9Ng
1. May a person concurrently act as treasurer for an elected
state offi-cer's political action conmittees and sit on the board
of directors of ROAR, a political action cornmittee formerly
controlled by the sane elected state officer? 
Does such activity
create a presumption of control of ROAR by the elected state
ot f acer ?
2. If the above activity is permissible, are there any
restrictions as to the types of decisions in which the treasurer
can participate? 
For example, may-the treasurer particioate in
deciiions concerning uraking extrrenditures to, or on behalf of, the
elected state officer for whom she is enployed?
2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
imrnunity provided by an opinion or fornal written advice.
(section 83113,' Regulation 18329(c) (3). )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 807 . sacramento cA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) 122'5660


Our Fi■e NoS. I-90-4■3 and I-90-55■
Page 2
3. 
Does the additional information contained in this letter
change the advice we gave in the former Brainerd Advice Letter,
No. A-9O-o39, concerning the status of RoAR as a candidate
controlled committee.
CONCLUSIONS
1. and 2. If the treasurer of an elected state officer's
controlled ccnmittees is a voting menber of another committee's
board of directors, there is a possibility that the elected
officer indirectly controls the committee. If the treasurer sits
on the other committee's board of directors on behalf of the
elected state officer, the possibility of j.ndirect cornmittee
control becomes a presumption. Therefore, under the facts
presented in this case, to ensure that RoAR is not characterized
as a committee controlled by the elected state officerr no agent
of the elected state officer or person actj.ng as a representative
of a committee controlled by the state officer nay sit on the
board of directors or participate in making decj.sions concerning
the expenditures of RoAR.
3. Soliciting funds on behalf of RoAR, by itself, 
does not
indicate control of RoAR. That is why we concluded that senator
Roberti could engage in such an activity on behalf of ROAR without
creating a presumption of controL. our conclusion assumed that
his degree of invoLvernent in the committee $ras so linited.
FACTS
senator Roberti founded and controlled an aninal rights
political 
action committee, Respect our AninaLs' Rights (rrRoARri).
Effective January l, ]-989, senator Roberti resigned frorn the
board of directors of RoAR.
Shirley wechsler sits on the board of directors of RoAR.
currently, there are only four members on the board of directors,
of which she is one. Shirley wechsler also is the treasurer for
Senator Roberti's political action cornmittees. She does not act
as a treasurer for any other elected official 
apart from Senator
Roberti and his political action comnittees. Ms. wechsler has
informed you that she sits on the board of directors of RoAR as a
private "itiz"r, 
and not on behalf of senator 'Roberti.
Initially, 
your inquiry was whether the (unnaned) treasurer
of political- action cornmittees of Senator Roberti rnay sit on the
board of directors of RoAR, or r'rhether her position as treasurer


Our Fi■e Nos. 
■-90-4■3 and I-90-55■
Page 3
hrould preclude her from such an acti-vity.3/ 
Subsequentlyr you
informed us that Shirley wechsler was the treasurer, that she
aLready was sitting on the board of directors of ROAR 7 and that
she had served in that capacity since the inception of ROAR.
ANALYSIS
As a general rule, a candidate controls a committee $/henever
the candidate has a significant infLuence on the actions or
deci.sions of the comrmitEee. (Section 82016.)
We look at the degree of the candidate,s involvernent in a
committee's activities to determine whether the candidate controls
the committee. (Madden Advice Letter, No. A-85-197, copy
enclosed. ) For that reason, although certain activities of a
candidate or elected official 
are not suffj-cient by themselves to
constitute contro], each is a factor to be considered in
deterrnining whether the candidate controls the committee. For
exanple, soliciting 
funds on behalf of a committee by itself 
would
not indicate control of the cornmittee. Other illustrations 
of
activities 
which are not sufficient by themselves to constitute
control of the committee are as follows: attending fundraising
actj.vi.ties of the committee, aIlo$ring the candidate,s or elected
official,s 
name on any type of letterhead of the committee, havingr
members of the candidate's or elected official,s 
controlled
comrnittee actively participate in the affairs of the committee,
or, accepting funds from the conmittee. Ho\rever, such activities
are relevant to whether such a committee is controlled by the
candidate or elected officiaL. 
(woodruff Advice Letter, No. I-89-
180, copy enclosed. ) 4/
The conmission has interpreted I'controlled committeerr very
broadly to incl-ude any significant participation in the actions of
a cornmittee by a candidate, his or her agent, or representative of
any other cornmittee he or she controls. Accordingly, we also look
3/ 
Your initial 
request for advice vras dated June 8, 1990. As a
result of ny telephone conversation with David Nagler, Executive
Vj-ce President of ROAR, a follow-up letter was sent to us on
July 10, 1990. Additional information was provided us by
telephone on August 21, 1-990 and November 5 and 6, 1990.
4/ That is why in our l,tarch 5, 1990 letter to you (Brainerd
Advice Letter, No. A-90-039, copy enclosed), vre concluded that
Senator Roberti could write a letter soliciting contributions on
behalf of the committee he had founded and formerLy controlled
without creating a presunption of control of the conrnittee,
provided that was the only action taken by him. Note that our
advice was premised in part on the fact that he had resigned from
the board of directors of this committee. we did not know at that
time that Senator Roberti's treasurer was sitting on the board of
dj.rectors of RoAR or that Senator Robertj.'s name appeared on the
Ietterhead of RoAR.


Our Fi■e Nos. 
■-90-4■3 and I-90-55■
Page 4
at the degree of the candidate's agent's or representative' s
i-nvolvement in the committee's activities to deterhine e/hether the
candidate controfs the committee. For example, if members of a
candidate's or elected official's 
own controlled committee are
active members of another political action committee, there is a
possibility that the candidate or elected official 
indirectly
controls the committee. (Hadden, suDra. ) If, on behalf of the
elected officiaf, 
mernbers of a candidate's or el,ected official's
control-Led committee are voting menbers of a cornmittee's board of
directors, a presumption exists that the candj-date or elected
official 
indirectly controls the ccmmittee. (Ferquson Advice
Letter, No. A-86-044, copy enclosed.)
In your initial 
inquiry, you asked lrhether the treasurer of
Senator Robert's contro]led committees could serve as a member of
the board of directors of RoAR. The concern was raised that her
position as treasurer for Senator Roberti would preclude her from
such an involvement v/ith ROAR. Subsequently hle learned the
identity of Senator Robertj,'s treasurer as Shirley l{echsler, and
the fact she already was sitting on the board of directors of
RoAR. As you are aware, the Commission does not provide advice
reJ-ating to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b) (8) (A) -)
Therefoie, wL can only advise you as to lrhether it is pernissible
for },ts. wechsler to act as Senator Roberti's treasurer and
continue to sit on the board, and if so, whether there are any
restri-ctions concerning the types of committee decisions in which
she nay and may not participate in the future.
In order to determine whether Shirley wechsler may continue
to sit on the board of directors of RoAR, we first 
need to
ascertain whether ROAR is a committee controlled by her employer,
Senator Roberti, or, is, in fact, an independent political 
action
committee. Looking at the factors to measure a candidate's or
elected official's 
degree of involvernent, we note that senator
Roberti's name is proninently listed on the RoAR's stationery,
that senator Roberti solicits funds for RoAR comrnittee, that RoAR
presumably makes expenditures either to, or on behalf of, Senator
ioberti, lnd that the treasurer of senator Roberti's political
action committees sits on the board of directors of RoAR.
Arguably, these factors may be construed to indicate control by
senator Roberti .
Therefore, under the facts Presented in this cdse, to ensure
that RoAR is not characterized as a controlled committee, no
elected officer or candidate, no aqent of an elected officer, 
and
no person acting as a representative of a cornnittee controlled by
an elected officer or candidate nay sit on the board of directors
or participate in naking decisions concerning the expenditures of
RoAR. (Woodruff, supra. )
However, if Shirley wechsler is not acting on behalf of
Senator Roberti, but in her capacity as a private citizen, and if
Senator Roberti's 1evel, of i-nvolvenent with RoAR is not


Our Fi■e Nos. 
エー90-4■3 and I-90-55■
Page 5
SH:DS:aa
Enc■osures
significant, then Ms. wechsler may continue to sit on senator
Roberti's controll-ed committees as wel] as RoAR.
whether a candidate or elective officia] 
actually controls a
conmittee depends on the specific facts of each situation. 
The
Legal Division of the Commission does not act as a fact-finding
body .
I hope this letter provides you with the guidance you.
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
natter, please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.
S incerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General CounseL

%/
By: Deanne Stone
counsel, Legal Division

officer indirectly controls the committee. If the treasurer sits
on the other committee's board of directors on behalf of the
elected state officer, the possibility of j.ndirect cornmittee
control becomes a presumption. Therefore, under the facts
presented in this case, to ensure that RoAR is not characterized
as a committee controlled by the elected state officerr no agent
of the elected state officer or person actj.ng as a representative
of a committee controlled by the state officer nay sit on the
board of directors or participate in making decj.sions concerning
the expenditures of RoAR.
3. Soliciting funds on behalf of RoAR, by itself, 
does not
indicate control of RoAR. That is why we concluded that senator
Roberti could engage in such an activity on behalf of ROAR without
creating a presumption of controL. our conclusion assumed that
his degree of invoLvernent in the committee $ras so linited.
